plainMeaning: The Jina is gentler than the moon, brighter than the sun, lovelier than Indra, and firmer than the greatest mountain.
recitation: vimala-sasi kalāirea somaṁ, vitimira sūra karāirea teaṁ; tiasavai gaṇāirea ruvaṁ, dharaṇidhara ppavarāirea sāraṁ

wordByWord.etymology: vimala ('stainless') + sasi (Skt. śaśi, 'moon'); Prakrit śaśi → sasi.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the moon-comparison; its kalā (digit) is the thing surpassed.

wordByWord.etymology: kalā ('lunar digit; art, skill') + airea (Skt. atireka, 'excess, surpassing'); kalā-atireka → kalāirea.
wordByWord.notes: kalā carries the double sense of the moon's bright crescent and 'artistry'; the Jina exceeds both.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. soma/saumya, 'cool, gentle, moonlike'; accusative of respect — the quality in which he excels.
wordByWord.notes: Pairs the Jina's serenity with the moon's soothing coolness.

wordByWord.etymology: vi- ('away') + timira ('darkness'); 'free of / driving away gloom'.
wordByWord.notes: Epithet of the sun, transferred to the Jina who dispels the darkness of delusion.
The second of the four surpassed luminaries.
Same -airea refrain; here the sun's rays are exceeded.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tejas → Prakrit tea; accusative of respect.
wordByWord.notes: The Jina's spiritual lustre outshines solar light.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tridaśa-pati ('lord of the thirty[-three] gods') → tiasavai; tridaśa → tiasa, pati → vai.
wordByWord.notes: Standard Prakrit name for Indra, the crown of celestial beauty.
Even Indra together with his whole divine retinue is outshone.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rūpa → Prakrit ruva; accusative of respect.
wordByWord.notes: The third surpassing: bodily/spiritual loveliness exceeding Indra's.

wordByWord.etymology: dharaṇi ('earth') + dhara ('bearer') = 'that which upholds the earth', i.e. a mountain.
wordByWord.notes: The fourth standard of excellence — the unshakable mountain-lord.

wordByWord.etymology: pavara (Skt. pravara, 'foremost, best') + airea (atireka); the doubled pp- is a metrical conjunct preserved from the book.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies dharaṇidhara as 'the best of mountains', whose firmness is still exceeded.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sāra ('core, strength, substance') → Prakrit sāra; accusative of respect.
wordByWord.notes: Caps the quartet: the Jina's unmovable essence outdoes mountain-rock.

literalTranslation: Surpassing the spotless moon's digit in gentleness; surpassing the darkness-dispelling sun's rays in radiance; surpassing the host of Indra, lord of the gods, in beauty; surpassing the most excellent mountain in firm essence.
idiomaticTranslation: Gentler than the stainless moon's own light, brighter than the rays of the gloom-banishing sun, lovelier than Indra and all his shining throng, and firmer of essence than the greatest of mountains — such is the Jina.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza is where the hymn's four-surpassings motif is compressed into a single, tightly engineered line. Each quarter (pāda) follows an identical template — [object of comparison] + the refrain -āirea (atireka, 'surpassing') + [the quality in which he surpasses, in the accusative]: moon → soma (gentle coolness), sun → teja (radiance), Indra's host → rūpa (beauty), best-of-mountains → sāra (firm essence). The fourfold anaphora of -āirea is the verse's whole architecture: hearing 'kalāirea… karāirea… gaṇāirea… ppavarāirea' four times in succession drills the single claim — he exceeds every pinnacle the world can name. Two touches sharpen it. First, the comparisons rise in scale: from a celestial light (moon), to a greater light (sun), to a person (Indra) — and pointedly his whole gaṇa, his retinue, not Indra alone — to the most massive and immovable thing imaginable (the foremost mountain). Second, the chosen qualities are not random: coolness, light, beauty, and unshakable strength together sketch a complete being — soothing yet brilliant, beautiful yet adamantine. Set just before the following verse's drumbeat of 'ajiaṁ… ajiaṁ' (unconquered in strength, body, and tapas), this surpassing-praise lays the groundwork: one who outstrips moon, sun, Indra, and mountain is, by that very fact, ajita — unconquered.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes a standard Jain devotional logic: the Tīrthaṅkara is praised not as a rival deity but as transcending the very yardsticks — natural and divine — by which excellence is normally measured. Indra and the gods, however exalted, remain within saṁsāra and themselves venerate the Jina; placing the Jina above 'tiasavai' and his whole gaṇa quietly asserts the supremacy of the liberated soul over even the highest heavenly station. The closing sāra ('firm essence') outdoing the mountain points beyond physical metaphor to the Jina's nirjarā-won, unshakable equanimity — a steadiness no upasarga can move.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter can take the four images as a checklist for the mind being calmed in pratikramaṇa: be cooling like the moon (gentle speech), clarifying like the sun (dispelling one's own delusion), inwardly beautiful through virtue rather than form, and firm as a mountain in resolve. Chanting the fourfold -āirea, one aspires toward, not just admires, that surpassing.

crossReferences.text: Ajitaśānti Stava, vv.13–18 (the four-surpassings sequence: moon, sun, Indra, mountain-lord)
crossReferences.source: Nandiṣeṇasūri; Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Following verse (smaran-2-ajitshanti/16): 'satte a sayā ajiaṁ… esa ahaṁ thuṇāmi jiṇaṁ ajiaṁ' — the surpassing culminates in the 'unconquered' (ajita) litany

sources.title: Atireka — definitions (Indian Epigraphical Glossary; Sanskrit/Pali dictionaries)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming airea < atireka = 'excess, surpassing, going beyond', the verse's fourfold refrain -āirea.

sources.title: Tridaśapati — definitions (Sanskrit/Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying tiasavai = tridaśa-pati = 'lord of the (thirty-three) gods', i.e. Indra.

sources.title: Dharaṇīdhara — definitions (Sanskrit/Jainism/Buddhism)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming dharaṇidhara = 'earth-bearer' = mountain, the fourth standard of excellence.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries (sanskrit-lexicon.uni-koeln.de)
sources.consultedFor: Root senses of soma/saumya, tejas, rūpa, sāra, and pravara underlying the accusatives of respect.

notes: Verse text follows the book verbatim, including the doubled conjunct in 'ppavarāirea' and variant anusvāra; not normalized. Parsing: each pāda reads [comparison-object]-airea + [quality-in-accusative], the implied subject being the Jina (Ajita/Śānti). 'kalā' is rendered with its primary lunar sense ('digit/phase') while noting its secondary 'art/skill'. The four-surpassings motif itself is established in the preface (vv.13–18) and is not re-expounded here; this verse is its compact, single-line crystallization. Sandhi/word-division in the IAST recitation is editorial for chantability; the book prints the line with its own spacing and a daṇḍa.
